title: Quiz: Reinforcement Learning Fundamentals
description: Test your understanding of core reinforcement learning concepts

questions.question: What are the five components of a Markov Decision Process (MDP)?

options.text: States, Actions, Transitions, Rewards, Discount factor
States, Policies, Values, Gradients, Learning rate

questions.explanation: An MDP is defined by (S, A, P, R, γ): State space S, Action space A, Transition probabilities P, Reward function R, and discount factor γ.

questions.question: What does the discount factor γ control?

options.text: How much future rewards are valued relative to immediate rewards

questions.explanation: The discount factor γ ∈ [0,1] determines how much the agent values future rewards. γ=0 means only immediate rewards matter; γ close to 1 means future rewards are almost as valuable as immediate ones.

questions.question: What is the key difference between Q-learning and SARSA?

options.text: Q-learning uses value functions, SARSA uses policies
Q-learning is off-policy (uses max Q), SARSA is on-policy (uses actual next action)
Q-learning is faster, SARSA is more accurate
Q-learning works only for discrete actions, SARSA for continuous

questions.explanation: Q-learning uses the maximum Q-value for the next state (off-policy), while SARSA uses the Q-value of the actual next action taken (on-policy).

questions.question: What does the policy gradient theorem relate?

options.text: The gradient of expected return to log-policy gradients weighted by Q-values

questions.explanation: The policy gradient theorem: ∇J(θ) = E[∇log π(a|s) · Q(s,a)], relating the gradient of expected return to log-policy gradients weighted by action values.

questions.question: In TD learning, the agent waits until the end of an episode to update value estimates.
questions.explanation: False. TD learning updates value estimates after each step using bootstrapping (estimates based on other estimates), unlike Monte Carlo which waits until episode end.

questions.question: What is the purpose of epsilon in epsilon-greedy exploration?

options.text: To balance exploration (random actions) with exploitation (greedy actions)

questions.explanation: Epsilon controls the exploration-exploitation tradeoff: with probability ε, take a random action (explore); otherwise take the best known action (exploit).

questions.question: What is the Bellman equation used for?

options.text: To recursively define value functions in terms of immediate rewards and future values
To encode states into quantum circuits

questions.explanation: The Bellman equation defines V(s) or Q(s,a) recursively: V(s) = Σ π(a|s) Σ P(s'|s,a)[R + γV(s')], relating current values to future values.

questions.question: Which are potential advantages of quantum computing for RL? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Quantum circuits can represent complex policy distributions
Superposition enables exploring multiple actions simultaneously
Quantum guarantees optimal policies in polynomial time

questions.explanation: Quantum RL potential includes: (a) expressive quantum policies, (b) entanglement for correlations, (c) superposition for exploration. However, (d) is false - no such guarantee exists.
